back: Graph colouring is the process of assigning colours to vertices of a graph such that no two adjacent vertices share the same colour. A proper colouring requires at least one colour per vertex with adjacent vertices having different colours.
The chromatic number χ(G) is the minimum number of colours required to properly colour a graph G. It is the smallest integer k such that G has a proper k-colouring.
A proper colouring of a graph G = (V, E) is a function c: V → {1, 2, ..., k} such that for every edge (u, v) ∈ E, c(u) ≠ c(v). No two adjacent vertices receive the same colour.

front: What is a colour class in graph colouring?
back: A colour class is the set of all vertices that receive the same colour in a proper colouring. Each colour class forms an independent set (no two vertices in the same class are adjacent).
The Four Color Theorem states that every planar graph can be properly coloured using at most four colours. This was the first major theorem proved using computer assistance in 1976.

front: What is the chromatic number of a complete graph Kₙ?
back: The chromatic number of a complete graph Kₙ is n (χ(Kₙ) = n). Since every pair of vertices is adjacent, all vertices require different colours.

front: What is the chromatic number of a bipartite graph?
back: The chromatic number of a bipartite graph is 2 (χ(G) = 2), except for isolated vertices. All bipartite graphs are 2-colourable.

Greedy colouring orders vertices in some sequence and assigns each vertex the smallest available colour that doesn't conflict with already coloured neighbours. The number of colours used depends on the vertex ordering.

front: What is a maximal planar graph?
back: A maximal planar graph (triangulated planar graph) is a planar graph to which no edge can be added without losing planarity. Every face (including outer) is a triangle.

front: What is the Five Color Theorem?
back: The Five Color Theorem (proved by Kempe in 1879) states that every planar graph can be properly coloured using at most five colours.
The chromatic polynomial P(G, k) counts the number of ways to properly colour a graph G using exactly k colours. It is a polynomial in k of degree |V|.
An optimal colouring is a proper colouring that uses the minimum possible number of colours, equal to the chromatic number χ(G). It uses exactly χ(G) colours.
Brooks' Theorem states that for any connected graph G that is not a complete graph or an odd cycle, the chromatic number χ(G) ≤ Δ(G), where Δ(G) is the maximum degree.
The clique number ω(G) is the size of the largest complete subgraph (clique) in G. Always true: ω(G) ≤ χ(G).
A graph is k-colourable if it has a proper colouring using at most k colours. A graph with chromatic number χ(G) = k is k-chromatic.
